title: contingency-dsl Respondent AST
description: AST schema for the respondent (two-term, CS-US) layer. Defines the Tier A primitives (R1-R14) plus the extension-primitive discriminator used by third-party respondent registries (primarily contingency-respondent-dsl). Paradigm-neutral shared types (Identifier, Number, Duration, Probability) are $ref'd from schema/foundations/types.schema.json.

RespondentExpr.description: Root discriminator across Tier A primitives and extension primitives.

StimulusRef.description: Reference to a CS or US. Either a let-bound identifier or a string label (e.g. "tone", "shock").

Duration.description: Time-valued quantity with an explicit unit. Mirrors schema/foundations/types.schema.json#/$defs/Duration.

PairForwardDelay.description: R1. CS onset precedes US onset; CS continues through US onset (temporal overlap). Pavlov (1927).

PairForwardTrace.description: R2. CS offset precedes US onset; a trace gap separates them. Ellison (1964).

PairSimultaneous.description: R3. CS onset equals US onset. Rescorla (1980).

PairBackward.description: R4. US onset precedes CS onset. Spetch, Wilkie, & Pinel (1981). Argument order in source text is (us, cs, isi) to match the temporal order of the canonical procedure.

Extinction.description: R5. CS-alone presentations after an acquisition history. Pavlov (1927); Bouton (2004). The prior history is a property of the enclosing PhaseSequence; a bare Extinction outside a phase context is a Phase 2 semantic error unless the program assigns a default history.

CSOnly.description: R6. CS presentations without US. Phase-independent (no prior acquisition required). Lubow & Moore (1959).

USOnly.description: R7. US presentations without CS. Phase-independent. Randich & LoLordo (1979).

Contingency.description: R8. A point in the Rescorla (1967) contingency space. p_us_given_cs is the probability of US given CS; p_us_given_no_cs is the probability of US given the absence of CS. Argument order (CS-conditional first) is fixed and NOT permutable.

TrulyRandom.description: R9. Syntactic sugar for a point on the diagonal Contingency(p, p) of the Rescorla contingency space. The optional p parameter fixes the shared probability; when omitted, the program may leave p unresolved or provide a default (Rescorla, 1967; Ayres, Benedict, & Witcher, 1975).

ExplicitlyUnpaired.description: R10. Contingency(0, p) with an additional temporal-separation constraint on US placement (Ayres, Benedict, & Witcher, 1975).

Compound.description: R11. Presents multiple CSs. Default mode is Simultaneous; additional modes are deferred to extension primitives.

Serial.description: R12. Presents CSs in temporal order. Does NOT accept a mode argument — seriality already implies temporal ordering.

ITI.description: R13. Intertrial-interval structural element. Jitter is a separate concern handled by the @iti annotation (schema/annotations/extensions/respondent-annotator.schema.json).

Differential.description: R14. Differential conditioning: contrastive training of CS+ versus CS-. Explicit form names all three of (cs_positive, cs_negative, us); short form omits us and relies on the enclosing @us annotation. Pavlov (1927); Mackintosh (1974).

ExtensionPrimitive.description: Respondent extension point. Third-party packages register Tier B primitives (blocking, overshadowing, renewal, etc.) whose names are resolved against the program's respondent registry at Phase 2. The grammar admits any UpperIdentifier-named constructor with a bracket-balanced argument list; registry resolution and per-primitive schema validation are the extension's responsibility.

items.description: Positional argument. Accepts any AnnotationValue-style scalar or structured value.

params.description: Keyword arguments; each value may be a scalar or a structured value.
